titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Lillian Spender, 29 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender gives an account of a recent ride he went for on his horse, Fanny. Midway through the ride, he realised that he had 'demoted' himself to captain (from Colonel) by accidentally wearing the wrong epaulette, and so had to speed home by a circuitous route to avoid social embarrassment. Spender tells Lillian that he still cannot get used to his recent promotion.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
July 29 My Pr I may have a busier day tomorrow
& consequently less time to write so
I will begin now after dinner tho'
with very little to say. It was a
hot day and I went out for a ride
on Fanny. Douglas puts away my
old coat saying you would not approve
of it any more, but it was rather
thinner than my others so I dug it
out for the ride. It struck me as
I went along that I was greeted a
little more casually than had lately
been the case, but Fanny was far
too interesting company to induce me
to take real notice of it. Suddenly
I glanced at my shoulder & saw
I had 'demoted' myself into a Captain
again. My ride home was a skilful
attempt to go fast past all I met
& to arrange myself between them &
the sun. Fanny could not understand
these manoeuvres at all but
wanted to prance home full speed all
the way which could not be permitted.
She probably thought me
quite mad when I passed the
obvious road & and took her down
a steep and precipitous path back
to behind the Church & so to my
house. Can't you see a b w slinking
in? Owing to the fact that most of
the Ulster guards used to present arms to
me this is not a surprise but I cannot
yet get over the being addressed as Crl
without a start. I don't altogether like
it. No letter today from you so I
shall close off. I hope you are flattered
by the difference in mine when there
is something to reply to! I hear the mail train has
broken down so I suppose I mustn't say
its pr's fault if I get no letter. I won't
continue just to flatter her Your b w
WB Spender
